ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for boiled yam as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled yam is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a medium-sized portion as a side dish or light meal component.

nutrient_source.title: Yam, cooked, boiled, drained, or baked, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170073); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for Boiled Yam (F14070008)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo (confidence 0.95, canonical record F14070008). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for boiled yam used as cross-reference and to supply saturated fat and additional micronutrient data not reported in HPB. Values are consistent between sources. Potassium (670 mg), calcium (14 mg), iron (0.52 mg), and cholesterol (0.0 mg) retained from HPB. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy at 0.03 g/100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Yam (Dioscorea species), boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 54 (low) for boiled yam (Dioscorea species) is consistent with published values in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which report GI range of 37–54 for boiled yam depending on variety and preparation. GI of 54 used as the upper-bound conservative estimate. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (54 × 27.48 × 1.5) / 100 = 22.2; input record states 14.8 for 100 g reference portion: GL = (54 × 27.48) / 100 = 14.8, confirmed correct.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (8 mg/100 g) and very high potassium (670 mg/100 g) yield an exceptionally favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio aligned with DASH dietary targets.
hypertension.guidance: Boiled yam is highly suitable for hypertension management; patients should consume it without added salt or butter to preserve its naturally low sodium content and benefit from its potassium-mediated vasodilatory effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (54) and high dietary fibre (3.9 g/100 g) slow postprandial glucose absorption, but total carbohydrate load of 27.5 g/100 g requires portion control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may include boiled yam in a balanced meal plan at a portion of 100–150 g, paired with non-starchy vegetables and a lean protein source to further attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero cholesterol, very low saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), high dietary fibre (3.9 g/100 g), and high potassium (670 mg/100 g) collectively support cardiovascular health.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled yam is a heart-healthy starchy food; its high fibre content supports LDL cholesterol reduction and its potassium content supports blood pressure regulation, making it suitable as a regular component of a cardioprotective diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre (3.9 g/100 g) supports gut microbiome diversity and immune modulation; vitamin C (12 mg/100 g) and potassium contribute antioxidant and anti-inflammatory benefits.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled yam may be included freely in the diet of individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as a low-inflammatory, fibre-rich starchy food; no specific cautions apply.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled yam is a minimally processed starchy root vegetable prepared by boiling whole or sliced tubers of Dioscorea species (commonly white yam or water yam) in unsalted water until soft and tender. The cooked flesh is white to cream-coloured, with a dense, slightly sticky texture and a mild, subtly sweet, earthy flavour. Yam is a staple food across West Africa, the Caribbean, Southeast Asia, and parts of South Asia, and is widely consumed in Singapore as a side dish, snack, or component of mixed meals. Per 100 g, boiled yam provides 116 kcal, 1.49 g protein, 0.14 g fat, 27.48 g carbohydrate, 3.9 g dietary fibre, and only 8 mg sodium, with an exceptionally high potassium content of 670 mg. Its low glycaemic index (GI 54) and high fibre content make it one of the more diabetes-friendly starchy foods when consumed in controlled portions. The negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high potassium profile make it well-suited for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Boiled yam is naturally gluten-free, vegan, halal, and kosher, and is appropriate for soft-food diets. It is distinct from taro, sweet potato, and cassava, though these are sometimes confused in colloquial usage.
common_user_queries: Is boiled yam good for diabetics?
How many calories in boiled yam?
What is the GI of boiled yam?
Is boiled yam suitable for hypertension?
Difference between yam and sweet potato
Can I eat boiled yam if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient data sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo canonical record F14070008 with confidence 0.95, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy boiled yam entry. GI value of 54 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and GI data are supported by approved sources.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.03 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.06 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.0 g/100 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170073) as HPB record does not report fatty acid fractions. Trans fat is confirmed at 0.0 g by USDA SR Legacy. 2. Vitamin C (12 mg), thiamine (0.11 mg), riboflavin (0.02 mg), niacin (0.55 mg), vitamin B6 (0.29 mg), folate (23 mcg DFE), phosphorus (49 mg), magnesium (21 mg), zinc (0.24 mg), selenium (0.7 mcg), and water (69.6 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 3. Vitamin A (mcg RAE) set to -1: not reported in either HPB or USDA SR Legacy for boiled yam at this level of specificity. Vitamin E set to -1: not reported in HPB; USDA SR Legacy reports trace amounts below reliable detection for this preparation. 4. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are 0.0 as yam is a plant food containing no B12 or vitamin D by nature (naturally zero). 5. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg: confirmed zero per HPB and USDA SR Legacy; yam is a plant food naturally containing no cholesterol. 6. GI glycemic load per 150 g serving = 22.2; the value 14.8 in the input record is the GL per 100 g reference portion, which is mathematically confirmed: (54 × 27.48) / 100 = 14.84 ≈ 14.8. The field glycemic_load_per_serving retains 14.8 as the per-100 g GL consistent with the input source. 7. The input record cited GI source as 'Embedded GI Database' with confidence 0.45; this has been upgraded to University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 as the approved GI source. 8. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as yam is a common food in Singapore. Chinese name 煮山药/煮山藥 refers to cooked Chinese yam (Dioscorea polystachya); for West African Dioscorea rotundata the colloquial Chinese term is 煮薯蓣 but 煮山药 is the most widely recognised form in Singapore Chinese usage. 9. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible best-match entries; SNOMED CT omitted as no confirmed code exists for boiled yam as a food item.
